title: Putin escalates ballistic missile attacks as Patriot shortages leave Ukraine defenseless
gist: Russia is on track to launch over a thousand ballistic missiles in 2026 (a ~15x jump from 2023) exploiting a global Patriot interceptor shortage worsened by the Iran war, while Ukraine scrambles for co-production, European alternatives, and cheaper homegrown interceptors that experts doubt can replace the Patriot soon.

insights.claim: If current trends continue, Russia is on track to launch more than a thousand ballistic missiles in 2026, a nearly fifteenfold increase compared to 2023.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Exponential growth in Russian ballistic volume implies sustained demand for interceptors and air-defense capacity far beyond current Western production.

insights.claim: Ukraine now regularly shoots down roughly 90 percent of drones and 80 percent of cruise missiles but less than one-third of ballistic missiles, which fly at much faster speeds and higher altitudes.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: The interception gap is entirely in ballistics, making PAC-3-class interceptors the single most valuable and supply-constrained asset in the war.
insights.locator: Described by Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy

insights.claim: During the first week of July 2026, Ukraine's air defenses intercepted just four of 49 incoming ballistic missiles, as two large-scale Russian attacks on the Kyiv region killed at least 57 civilians.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: An 8 percent ballistic interception rate shows Ukrainian defenses are near collapse against this threat, raising urgency and price for any interceptor supply.
insights.locator: The situation is only getting worse

insights.claim: Russia has adopted a new bombing strategy in which concentrated bombardments aimed at one primary target feature dozens of ballistic missiles alongside hundreds of drones and other missile types to overwhelm Ukrainian defenses.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Saturation tactics defeat point defenses regardless of interceptor quality, favoring mass-producible cheap interceptors and layered defense over premium systems alone.
insights.locator: For the first time, concentrated Russian bombardments

insights.claim: Kyiv has received at least ten Patriot batteries and more than six hundred advanced interceptors since April 2023, with roughly 70 percent of interceptor deliveries over the past year coming via NATO's Prioritized Ukraine Requirements List (PURL) mechanism.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: PURL is the dominant delivery channel; its funding and continuation are the key variable for Ukrainian air-defense supply.

insights.claim: Even if Ukraine received all 620 Patriot interceptor missiles Lockheed Martin produced last year, it would still fall far short of the estimated 2000 interceptors required per year to protect key civilian, energy, and military targets.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Demand exceeds total global PAC-3 output by more than 3x, an unbridgeable gap without a step-change in production capacity.
insights.locator: It is not only a question about financing

insights.claim: The US-Israeli war in Iran reportedly consumed as many as 1430 Patriot munitions in just 39 days, further exacerbating the global interceptor supply crisis.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Simultaneous Middle East demand directly competes with Ukraine for the same scarce interceptors, tightening the global squeeze.
insights.locator: The US-Israeli war in Iran has further exacerbated

insights.claim: Even with plans to triple Patriot production, US allies ordering new systems currently face delivery timelines of up to seven years, and each PAC-3 missile takes more than two years to build across a supply chain of over four hundred companies.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Multi-year lead times and a 400-supplier chain mean no near-term supply relief; the bottleneck is industrial capacity, a durable investment theme for defense suppliers.
insights.locator: Even with plans to triple production

insights.claim: Washington recently granted preliminary approval to produce PAC-3 missiles in Poland, and Trump said after the G7 summit he was open to American companies manufacturing Patriot interceptors under license in Ukraine.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: European and Ukrainian co-production would expand interceptor supply over years; watch for firm agreements as leading indicators of relief.
insights.locator: In a positive first step, Washington recently granted

insights.claim: Ukraine is pursuing direct PAC-3 purchase contracts with EU funds, acquiring older Patriot variants from European stockpiles, and negotiating with countries to defer their place in the production queue for earlier Ukrainian delivery.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Queue-jumping and EU-funded direct buys show demand competition among allies; secondary-market and older-variant interceptors become valuable stopgaps.
insights.locator: To help close the gap, Ukrainian Defense Minister

insights.claim: The only existing alternative to the Patriot is the French-Italian SAMP/T, which faces limited production capacity and cannot engage Russian ballistics in its current version, though Ukraine is set to test the next-generation SAMP/T NG later this year.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: The SAMP/T NG test is a key catalyst; success would create a non-US anti-ballistic option and diversify European air defense procurement.
insights.locator: At present, the only existing alternative

insights.claim: Ukraine's FirePoint and Estonia's Frankenburg Technologies are each rushing to build lower-cost, mass-produced interceptors, but experts are skeptical these can replace the Patriot anytime soon.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Cheap mass-produced interceptor startups are the long-term answer to saturation attacks; early-stage bets with uncertain timelines to counter ballistics.
insights.locator: Meanwhile, Ukraine's FirePoint and Estonia's Frankenburg

insights.claim: The Ukrainian-made Lima electronic warfare system jams and spoofs satellite navigation signals, fooling incoming ballistic missiles into thinking they are in Peru so they veer off course, though diverted missiles can still cause significant damage.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Low-cost electronic warfare offers a partial, cheap counter to guided ballistics, a complement to expensive kinetic interceptors worth watching for scalability.
insights.locator: Another innovative tool already in use

insights.claim: Ukraine is increasingly seeking to 'shoot the archer, not the arrow' with record long-range strikes on Russian military-industrial targets, including a semiconductor plant in Voronezh, a ballistic launcher hub in Volgograd, and a missile sensor factory in Penza.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Attacking missile production at source is Ukraine's cost-effective supply-side counter; degraded Russian component plants could slow ballistic output.
insights.locator: As Russia expands its ballistic arsenal
